coredata.dc:description: Dual emission ratiometric fluorescence probes have been widely used for naked visual individual detection of hazardous chemicals, but it is still limited to simultaneously detecting multiple targets in a complex system. In this work, a deep learning-assisted smartphone-integrated tricolor ratiometric fluorescence optical device has been designed for visual monitoring of Cu2+ and thiram. The tricolor sensing probes are consist of blue-emission carbon quantum dots (B-QDs), green-emission cadmium telluride quantum dots (G-QDs), and red-emission cadmium telluride quantum dots (R-QDs). The sensing system presents a three emission response to Cu2+ and thiram based on electron transfer effect, complexing effect, and inner filter effect (IFE), respectively. The reaction mechanisms were verified by density functional theory (DFT). Interestingly, the G-QDs and R-QDs’ fluorescence intensity are simultaneously quenched by Cu2+, whereas the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs remained unchanged and used as the internal reference, resulting in a distinct color shift from orange-red to blue with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.05 μM. Subsequently, the addition of thiram restored the G-QDs and R-QDs’ intensity while the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs was quenched accompanied by a distinguishable color transition from blue to red with LOD of 0.073 μM. Moreover, colorimetric detection of thiram is realized based on the variation of UV absorption intensity with LOD of 0.142 μM. Besides, deep learning-YOLO v3 algorithm-assisted smartphone-integrated tricolor portable optical device for in-situ monitoring of Cu2+ and thiram with ultralow LOD of 0.344 μM and 1.840 μM, respectively. In conclusion, the sensing system shows excellent stability, sensitivity, and specificity, which provides a great capacity for the convenient evaluation of Cu2+ concentration and thiram residue.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors wish it to be known that, in their opinions, Zhiwei Lu and Maoting Chen should be regarded as joint First Authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A self-designed smartphone-based portable device and system for detecting Cu2+ and thiram by multi-color ratiometric fluorescence optical probe based on deep learning and density functional theory.

Dual emission ratiometric fluorescence probes have been widely used for naked visual individual detection of hazardous chemicals, but it is still limited to simultaneously detecting multiple targets in a complex system. In this work, a deep learning-assisted smartphone-integrated tricolor ratiometric fluorescence optical device has been designed for visual monitoring of Cu2+ and thiram. The tricolor sensing probes are consist of blue-emission carbon quantum dots (B-QDs), green-emission cadmium telluride quantum dots (G-QDs), and red-emission cadmium telluride quantum dots (R-QDs). The sensing system presents a three emission response to Cu2+ and thiram based on electron transfer effect, complexing effect, and inner filter effect (IFE), respectively. The reaction mechanisms were verified by density functional theory (DFT). Interestingly, the G-QDs and R-QDs’ fluorescence intensity are simultaneously quenched by Cu2+, whereas the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs remained unchanged and used as the internal reference, resulting in a distinct color shift from orange-red to blue with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.05 μM. Subsequently, the addition of thiram restored the G-QDs and R-QDs’ intensity while the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs was quenched accompanied by a distinguishable color transition from blue to red with LOD of 0.073 μM. Moreover, colorimetric detection of thiram is realized based on the variation of UV absorption intensity with LOD of 0.142 μM. Besides, deep learning-YOLO v3 algorithm-assisted smartphone-integrated tricolor portable optical device for in-situ monitoring of Cu2+ and thiram with ultralow LOD of 0.344 μM and 1.840 μM, respectively. In conclusion, the sensing system shows excellent stability, sensitivity, and specificity, which provides a great capacity for the convenient evaluation of Cu2+ concentration and thiram residue.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, pesticide residues and heavy metal ions are common pollutants, which have potential harm to human health and environmental safety [1]. Copper ion (Cu2+) is the essential trace element for the body, but high concentration of Cu2+ will cause serious consequences for diseases [2]. However, due to the wide application of Cu2+, its pollution assessment is still a very important issue [3]. Moreover, as a special commodity to control the harmful biological hazards of agricultural and forestry crops, such as diseases, insects, grass, and rats, pesticides play an extremely important role in protecting agricultural production, improving agricultural comprehensive production capacity, promoting stable grain production and farmers' sustainable income. They are indispensable means of production and disaster relief materials for modern agriculture [4]. Among them, thiram is a compound of N, N-dialkyl dithiocarbamate, which has been widely used in the field or storage protection of crops and vegetables. However, excess thiram enters the soil, water, atmosphere, and organisms, and remains in the human body through bioaccumulation and food chain, which leads to a series of pesticide pollution problems, seriously endangers human health, and destroys the balance of the agricultural ecological environment [5]. It has been reported that thiram can lead to the death of lymphocytes, chondrocytes, and somatic cells [6]. Therefore, the test of Cu2+ and thiram has attracted widespread attention.

So far, great progress has been made in the quantitative research and analysis of Cu2+ and thiram. Common analytical methods for the detection of Cu2+ and thiram include atomic absorption [7], fluorescence spectroscopy [8], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [9], surface-enhanced raman spectroscopy (SERS) [10,11], chemiluminescence [12], localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [13,14], and electrochemical methods [15]. Among the above methods, the fluorescence method is considered the most promising technique because of its high sensitivity and selectivity [8]. Moreover, fluorescence sensing is a simple, fast and effective analysis method because it does not require complex sample preparation before analysis [8]. Interestingly, fluorescence sensing can be constructed in “turn on”, “turn off” and “ratiometric” response types [16,17]. At present, several fluorescence sensors for the determination of Cu2+ or thiram have been developed [18,19]. The construction of these fluorescent sensor systems mainly depends on fluorescent nanomaterials, including metal nanoclusters (MNCs), quantum dots (QDs), luminescent metal–organic frameworks (M−MOFs), etc. [20].

However, among many fluorescence sensors, single emission fluorescence probes with independent fluorescence signals are sensitively influenced by probe concentration, instrument noise level, and sample environment, leading to no obvious color change. In contrast, the construction of dual-color fluorescent probes based on ratio fluorescence sensing can significantly enhance the color contrast and improve the detection sensitivity [21]. Our group is very interested in designing and fabricating dual-emission ratio fluorescence sensors for naked visualization detection of Cu2+, ascorbic acid, and uric acid. For example, Rao et al., constructed a double emission fluorescent probe C-dot@SiO2@Q-dots with SiO2 as the connecting coating for detecting Cu2+ [22]. Lu et al., reported Fe-MIL-88NH2/Au-NCs ratio fluorescent composites to measure Hg2+ and thiram [23]. Unfortunately, the dual emission probe not only has a complex manufacturing process but also has little color change [24]. To solve this problem, multicolor-variation fluorescence sensing systems have great potential in practical application. So far, the fluorescence detection methods for Cu2+ and thiram are mainly single emission or double emission fluorescence detection, without obvious color change [22,23], and these detection strategies not only face the problem of poor detection effect, but also need to use large instruments and equipment, which can not be well popularized and applied in real life and can not realize the basic research to real life.

The detection efficiency is one of the indicators that must be considered in the practical detection of Cu2+ and thiram, so rapid detection, a simplified operation process, and portable equipment are needed. The portable test platform is promising. fluorescence and colorimetric detection based on a smartphone have been widely used in the test of toxic and harmful substances [25]. In terms of functions, the smartphone is more like a handheld computer. Combined with digital imaging and the powerful computing power of the smartphone, mobile phones with camera devices can completely obtain high-resolution images and carry out subsequent image analysis. What is exciting is that the rapid development of smartphones gives us more opportunities to simplify traditional sensing measurement tools with smartphones. Besides, another advantage of smartphones is that they can be used as a signal reader to install third-party service providers or self-developed programs. The critical point lies in the fact that the application of automatic chromaticity reading is to exploit a yare identification unit as an accessory and integrate it into the smartphone [26]. The built-in camera of the smartphone is used as an optical sensing element to obtain the material content through image processing technology. Our research group has been developed a rapid colorimetric detection technology for metal ions and glutathione based on a smartphone combined with the ratiometric fluorescent sensor. Rao et al., synthesized a smartphone-based dual emission biomass carbon point fluorescence for the detection of Al3+ and H2O [27]. Liu et al., constructed a platform for ratio fluorescence colorimetric determination of H2O2 and glucose based on smartphone [28], and Liu et al., reported a portable smartphone platform to detect glutathione and Azodicarbonamide [29]. They have combined smartphones to realize the on-site detection of various substances. In recent years, how to combine the advantages of smartphones, fluorescent composite materials, and artificial intelligence is a worthy domain of study.

Deep learning is an important branch of artificial intelligence, and its study has extensive and far-reaching significance among lots of researchers. Deep machine learning algorithms can be used for classification and prediction tasks [30]. The end-to-end target detection method represented by the YOLO v3 model has the advantages of fast recognition speed, high recognition accuracy, and strong model anti-interference ability, and has the potential to recognize target objects in complex environments [31]. In addition, to the best of our knowledge, machine learning-assisted tricolor-variation-based ratio fluorescence-enhanced sensors for the detection of Cu2+ and thiram have not been reported.

Here, we report a smartphone and deep learning dual-assisted tri-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensing system for dose sensitive and visual detection of Cu2+ and thiram based on tricolor probes (blue: B, green: G, and red: R). By simply mixing with one blue carbon QDs (B-QDs, Scheme 1 a) and two glutathione (GSH) functionalized CdTe QDs (G-QDs and R-QDs, Scheme 1b) based on electrostatic interaction to form B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs composite materials (Scheme 1c). In the presence of Cu2+, the fluorescence intensities of R-QDs and G-QDs are simultaneously quenched, whereas the B-QDs are insensitive to Cu2+ and serve as the internal reference, resulting in a distinct color variation from orange-red to blue (Scheme 1c). However, the coordination between thiram and Cu2+ terminated the electron transfer between GSH-functionalized QDs and Cu2+, and restored the fluorescence intensity of R-QDs and G-QDs, leading to the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs being quenched accompanied by a distinguishable color transition from blue to red. The binding mode between the components of the composite was be verified based on density functional theory (DFT), and the competitive reaction mechanism between the detector and fluorescent sensor was also been explored. Besides, the colorimetric detection of thiram was realized based on the variation of UV absorption intensity. All color changes can be photographed and quantified with a smartphone's optical camera. In addition, YOLO v3-assisted deep learning for smartphone cameras captures fluorescent colorimetric images, identifies and processes photos, and uses self-designed software to cut and read RGB values for indicating the concentration of Cu2+ and thiram in real samples (Scheme 1d). This sensing system realizes highly sensitive detection of Cu2+ in agricultural water, blood, and urine, as well as thiram in fruits, vegetables, and other agricultural products. The smartphone-integrated multi-color ratiometric fluorescence portable sensing platform provides a splendid application for the efficient and portable test of toxic and harmful substances. The merits of the tri-color fluorescence optical device are mainly as follows: (1) the tricolor probe can be prepared by simple mixing without other tedious steps; (2) it has excellent selectivity and ultra-high sensitivity to Cu2+ based on the rich and extensive color variation for visual eye observed; (3) the sensing system realizes the ratio and colorimetric dual-detection mode of thiram; (4) interestingly, the portable optical device integrated deep learning technology with a smartphone to capture the RGB values for in-situ monitoring of Cu2+ and thiram.

The reagents, instruments, and material synthesis steps are displayed in the
Supporting information
.

In order to detect Cu2+, B-QDs (16 µL), G-QDs (15 µL) and R-QDs (16 µL) were dispersed into 1933 µL phosphate buffer (pH = 6.0). After reaction for 1 min at 40℃, the fluorescence spectra in the wavelength range of 380 ∼ 700 nm were recorded under the excitation of ultraviolet light with the wavelength of 365 nm. the slit widths of emission and excitation are 10 nm and 15 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric detection of thiram
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of thiram, the thiram solution (50 µL, different concentrations) was mixed with Cu2+ solution (20 µL, 100 μM) at room temperature for 10 min to obtain solution A. Then, B-QDs (16 µL), G-QDs (15 µL), and R-QDs (16 µL) were dispersed into 1883 µL phosphate buffer (pH = 6.0) to obtain solution B. Finally, after mixing solution A and B for 1 min at 40℃, the fluorescence spectrometer of the sample under the same setting conditions as Cu2+ detection. In addition, the content of thiram can also be detected by testing the absorbance of the sample.

To estimate the practicability of the tri-emission system as a fluorescent probe to detect Cu2+ and thiram in actual samples, agricultural water, urine, blood, peaches, and cabbage were selected as the research objects, among them, peaches and cabbages need to be juiced firstly, and agricultural water, urine, blood, peach juice, and cabbage juice were then diluted 10 or 100 times and used filter membrane with 0.22 μm before sensing detection, and then detect Cu2+ and thiram in the sample. Using the standard addition recovery method to evaluate the sensitivity of the tricolor fluorescence system to the detection of Cu2+ and thiram in the actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence and colorimetric dual-sensing mechanisms of the tricolor probe
ce:section.ce:para: The tricolor quantum dots (B-QDs, G-QDs, and R-QDs) are physically mixed and assembled into a ratio-based B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs (orange-red) fluorescence sensor, which can intuitively and sensitively detect Cu2+ and thiram. The ratio fluorescent composites have three emission peaks at λem = 429 nm, λem = 518 nm, and λem = 625 nm under the excitation of 365 nm light. The fluorescence intensity at 518 nm and 625 nm are linearly quenched with the increase of Cu2+ concentration based on the electron transfer effect, while the intensity at 429 nm is almost unchanged. Therefore, the fluorescence peaks at 518 nm and 625 nm as the detection signals, and the fluorescence peaks at 429 nm the internal standard, thus the B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs sensor (△F = (F625 + F518)/F429) was used to measure the Cu2+ signal. At the same time, under ultraviolet light (λem = 365 nm), as the Cu2+ concentration increases, the fluorescence color gradually changes from orange-red to purple-red, purple-blue, and blue (Fig. S1a).

However, in the system of B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs coexisting with Cu2+, when thiram is present, both fluorescence intensities of R-QDs and G-QDs recovered and increased with the increase of thiram concentration based on the complexing effect, whereas the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs exhibits gradual decreases as the thiram concentration increasing based on IFE. Therefore, under ultraviolet light (λex = 365 nm), as the concentration of thiram increases, the fluorescence color of the device gradually changes from blue to purple-red, orange-red, and red (Fig. S1b). Besides, the color of the system becomes brownish yellow, the colorimetric detection of thiram is realized based on the variation of UV absorption intensity. According to the above principle, the colorimetric and fluorescence dual-signal platform can detect the content of Cu2+ and thiram.

Smartphones become more important and unique due to their mobility and portability. In addition, smartphones are equipped with high-definition cameras and editable programs. It is a relatively emerging technology to transform mobile phones into sensing and measuring platforms [23,29]. The smartphone-based colorimetric sensing mechanism is attributed to the camera's recognition of different color photos, extracting RGB values, and the fitting of the linear relationship between RGB values and target concentration for indicating the concentration of Cu2+ and thiram in real samples.

We propose a machine learning-based strategy that automatically recognizes photos to predict RGB values related to the content of the object. The research process is divided into two procedures. The first procedure focuses on image feature processing and extraction: preprocessing, such as clipping, channel selection, and average intensity of RGB acquired images. A particularly important task is to identify areas of interest so that the location of the cuvette can be correctly detected. The second procedure combines artificial neural network models for classification and prediction tasks. All the software is designed by ourselves, and the sample library extraction code of deep learning is as follows: https://github.com/tankWang1024/iechemistry.

R-QDs, G-QDs, and B-QDs’ UV–vis absorption spectra were displayed in Fig. 1 a. the peaks of R-QDs, G-QDs, and B-QDs are 552.0 nm, 487.0 nm, and 526.0 nm respectively. The surface-functionalized groups of trichromatic quantum dots can be investigated by using FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b), the peaks near at 1600 cm−1, 2600–2500 cm−1, 3300 cm−1, and 3500–3300 cm−1 represent the bending vibrations of C=O, S-H, O–H, and NH3 groups, respectively. The characteristic absorption band at 3253 cm−1 and 3407 cm−1 be characteristic of the bending vibration of N–H and O–H, respectively, and the peak at 1399 cm−1 comes from the bending vibration of C-N. The peak at 1593 cm−1 is rooted in the asymmetric bending vibration of carboxylate anions. The disappearance of the vibration of the S-H group at 2525 cm−1 of CdTe QDs may be caused by the formation of a complex between mercaptan and Cd atoms on the surface of CdTe QDs, and there is no stretching vibration of S-H in B-QDs, indicating that s volatilizes during the high-temperature reaction for the preparation of B-QDs so that there is no initial S-H on the surface [32]. In conclusion, the surface-rich hydrophilic groups can significantly improve the stability of B-QDs and CdTe QDs [33]. Fig. 1c is the XRD diagram of B-CDs, exhibited that the peak at 2θ = 20.0° represents its (311) plane, while G-QDs and R-QDs have obvious peaks at 2θ = 31.1°, 33.9°, 44.6°, 47.0° and 56.0° due to the same synthesis method (Fig. 1d), corresponding to the (200), (101) and (211) crystal planes, respectively. Above spectra data indicate that R-QDs, G-QDs, and B-QDs are successfully fabricated.

The morphologies and size of R-QDs, G-QDs, and B-QDs were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2
(a-b), B-QDs presents approximate spherical dots, and the average size of 5.7 nm ( Fig. 2c), and enlarged images of the B-QDs by HRTEM show that the lattice spacing is 0.220 nm, corresponding to the (100) face of graphite [34]. As shown in Fig. 1 (d-i), both R-QDs and G-QDs exhibits great dispersion, and an average size of 3.3 nm and 3.9 nm, respectively. HRTEM images show the lattice spacing of 0.288 nm and 0.242 nm, corresponding to its (101) and (200) planes.

Fi…g. S2(a-c) is the XPS spectrum of all the as-prepared QDs XPS full spectrum of B-QDs exhibits five peaks at 531 eV, 400 eV, 285 eV, 163 eV, and 220 eV, which indicated that B-QDs was composed of C, O, N, and S elements (Fig. S2a). High-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. S3a) can be fitted with four peaks corresponding to C=C/C–C (284.8 eV), C-N (286.2 eV), C=O (287.9 eV), and –COOH (288.7 eV), respectively. O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S3b) possessed two peaks at 531.2 eV and 532.3 eV, corresponding to O–H and C=O groups, respectively [28]. High-resolution S 2p (Fig. S3c) spectrum shows two different binding energy at 164.2 eV and 163.0 eV, which was related to the contribution of C-S 2p1/2 and C-S 2p3/2, respectively [35].

Besides, the XPS spectra proved that Te, Cd, C, O, and S were contained in both G-QDS (Fig. S2b) and R-QDS (Fig. S2c). Fig. S4a shows the spectrum of C 1 s in has three peaks at 282.2 eV 286.0 eV, and 284.8 eV, belonging to C=O, C-N, and C–C/C=C groups respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s mainly exhibits two peaks at 530.9 eV(O–H) and 532.1 eV (C-OH/ C-O-C) in Fig. S4b. However, Cd 3d spectrum generally shows two peaks at 411.3 eV and 404.5 eV, corresponding to Cd in CdTe (Fig. S4c). Meanwhile, Te 3d spectrum peaks reflected at 571.9 eV and 582.1 eV in Fig. S4d, which were consistent with the Cd-Te bonding state [33]. Similar results are presented in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of R-QDs, as displayed in Fig. S4(e-h). Furthermore, the measured zeta potentials of R-QDs, G-QDs, and G-QDs are + 2.02 mV, −5.91 mV, and −5.01 mV, respectively, indicating that B-QDs is positively charged, and the surfaces of G-QDs and R-QDs are negatively charged (Fig. S2(d-f)). To sum up, G-QDs and R-QDs have the same element composition and similar surface information. Meanwhile, the surface of B-QDs is positively charged, while the CdTe QDs are negatively charged. Therefore, due to the existence of weak electrostatic attraction, the three-color fluorescent probes are combined to form a composite B-QDs/G-QDs/G-QDs multicolor fluorescent probe.

Condition optimization mainly includes the following parameters: (a) pH value of the reaction system; (b) reaction temperature; (c) Cu2+ reaction time and (d) thiram reaction time. The corresponding optimization methods and optimization data were given in the supporting materials. The optimal experimental conditions and test results are as follows: (a) the optimal pH value is 6.0; (b) the optimal reaction temperature is 40℃; (c) The best reaction time of Cu2+ is 1 min; (d) the best reaction time of thiram is 14 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and visual sensing of Cu2+ and thiram
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal test conditions, the fluorescence intensity ratio of ΔF has a good linear relationship with Cu2+ concentration in the range of 0.035–11 µM (R2 =0.9977) (Fig. 3
(a-b)), the lower detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.05 μM (S/N = 3). In addition, as the Cu2+ concentration increases, the intensity of fluorescence at 518 nm and 625 nm decreased gradually, from orange to purple, purple-blue, and blue. Fig. S6a shows the CIE 1931 (Chromaticity Coordinate Calculation) color diagram of Cu2+ titration, the fluorescence color changes from orange-red to blue as the Cu2+ concentration adding. Moreover, 3D emission profiles of the B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs system in Fig. S7a, it can also be seen that when Cu2+ exists, the green and red peaks are significantly reduced, while the blue peaks remain unchanged. Above results indicate that there is no mutual interference between R-QDs, G-QDs, and B-QDs, and the feasibility of the visual test of Cu2+ was verified. As displayed in Table 1 , the trichromatic B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs fluorescence system has a lower LOD value compared with the reported system, and the trichromatic probe has better visual recognition ability than the two-color or monochromatic probe in visual detection of Cu2+.

As the Cu2+ concentration increased, the ΔF values of fluorescent probes were gradually weakened. When the Cu2+ concentration exceeds 11 μM, the ΔF values reached a plateau. Therefore, the concentration of Cu2+ was set to 11 μM for the detection of thiram. Besides, the ΔF values of the system were gradually increased with the extension of the reaction time of thiram and Cu2+ and reached the maximum after 14 min. This was ascribed that thiram will complex Cu2+, resulting in the red and green fluorescent signals being restored and the blue fluorescent signal being reduced. Thus, the concentration of thiram is within range of 1.04–49.91 μM, the ΔF values have a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.9973) with the concentration of thiram (Fig. 3 (c-d)), and the LOD is calculated to be 0.073 μM. Fig. S6b shows the change of the color diagram after the addition of thiram. By comparing the 3D emission profiles of B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs in Fig. S7(b-c), it can also be seen that when Cu2+ and thiram exist at the same time, the green and red peaks recover obviously, while the blue peaks show a certain decrease. Thus, the tricolor probe has better visual recognition ability than the two-color or monochromatic probe in visual detection of thiram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism for the analysis of Cu2+ and thiram.
ce:section.ce:para: For the detection of Cu2+, the dual-quenching phenomenon of R-QDs and G-QDs was investigated in detail. When the Cu2+ solution was added to the quantum dot solution, the corresponding Zeta potential and XPS were measured after drying.

Fig. S2(e-f) proved that the original CdTe QDs (G-QDs, and G-QDs) surface is negatively charged. When the Cu2+ was addition the B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs system, the electrostatic attraction between Cu2+ and CdTe QDs led to the occurrence of the dual-quenching based on the electron transfer effect [27]. In addition, CdTe QDs are synthesized with GSH as a ligand, and many characteristic functional groups of GSH are retained on the surface of B-QDs (confirmed by FT-IR). It has been reported that GSH carboxyl has a strong coordination effect on Cu2+ [60,61]. Therefore, we believe that Cu2+ is easily adsorbed on B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs composite due to the existence of a large number of GSH carboxyl groups on the surface of R-QDs and G-QDs, which is more conducive to the subsequent reaction.

Fig. 4(a-c) shows the Cu 2P XPS spectra of the composite prepared by introducing Cu2+ into the quantum dot solution. Using XPS peak software to correct the original data of the XPS spectrum of Cu, it can be easily explained that two positions (952.3 eV and 932.5 eV) of Cu belong to Cu+, while the other two positions (954.5 eV and 934.5 eV) belong to Cu2+ [62]. The two orange peaks and two blue peaks are displayed in Fig. 4 are Cu2+ and Cu+. Because more Cu2+ were reduced by R-QDs or G-QDs, the intensity of the Cu2+ peak decreases with the increase of the amount of R-QDs or G-QDs in the system [63]. The sample of B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs represented in Fig. 4c contains higher concentration CdTe QDs (R-QDs and G-QDs), so when the same amount of Cu2+ was added, all Cu2+ was reduced and exists only in the form of Cu+. The incorporation of Cu2+ into B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs leads to surface defects, non-radiative recombination and fluorescence quenching of excitons, This can be attributed to cation exchange mechanism [64]. cation exchange is the quenching mechanism for Cu2+ to quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs. With the increase of the amount of Cu2+ addition, the dual-quenching effect becomes more obvious, this is because of the reduction of Cu2+ to Cu+ by electrons of the conduction band (CB). As the concentration of Cu2+ solution increases, more Cu+ will be generated and will replace Cd on the surface of CdTe QDs [65]. The related reactions are displayed in Fig. 4d occur on the surface of CdTe QDs. Cu+ accumulates surround the CdTe QDs surface, and then instead of Cd, compared with original CdTe QDs, whose energy level is lower. Cu2+ can get electrons from CB, so it can quench the photoluminescence intensity of CdTe QDs (Fig. 4e) [66]. Therefore, in this case, ultra-sensitive detection of Cu2+ can be realized [67].

Besides, we studied the fluorescence lifetime of B-QDs, R-QDs, and G-QDs in the presence or absence of Cu2+ (Fig. S8). After the addition of Cu2+, the fluorescence lifetimes of G-QDs (from 22.72 ns to 19.73 ns) and R-QDs (from 28.16 ns to 21.08 ns) were significantly shortened, while the fluorescence lifetime of B-QDs almost did not change, indicating that the quenching process of both R-QDs and G-QDs initiated by Cu2+ would be attributed to dynamic quenching [68].

For a better understanding of the binding effect between Cu2+ and thiram, the UV absorption spectra of Cu2+ solution and thiram solution were measured. As shown in Fig. 5 a, after adding Cu2+, thiram-Cu2+ exhibited an obvious absorption peak at about 420 nm. As demonstrated in the illustration in Fig. 5a, the solution changed from colorless and transparent to obvious yellow under sunlight. Besides, FT-IR spectra of thiram in the presence or absence of Cu2+ were also measured (Fig. 5b). Cu2+ binds to the large number of N–H, C-O, C-N, C=N, O–H, and others on the surface of thiram (the place marked by a, b, c, d in Fig. 5b). Moreover, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was used to characterize the combination of thiram and Cu2+ in PBS buffer with pH = 7.0 (Fig. 5c). The electrochemical response signal of thiram alone appeared at + 0.8 V (orange line), and there was no electrochemical signal of Cu2+ in the potential range of 0–1 V (green line) [69]. When Cu2+ was added to the thiram system, the electrochemical signal of thiram + Cu2+ at 0.8 V was disappeared. It may be that Cu2+ and thiram mixing formed a new complex, which leads to hinder the electron loss process of thiram. Meanwhile, the fluorescence curve changes of reaction with the tricolor probe when Cu2+, thiram, Cu2+ , and thiram co-exist at the same time are compared. As shown in Fig. S9, after the thiram complexed with Cu2+, the reaction between Cu2+ and the probe composite material was prevented, thus achieving fluorescence recovery.

The above results show that a new thiram-Cu2+ complex was formed between thiram and Cu2+, and the affinity between thiram and Cu2+ is much stronger than that of Cu2+ to the tricolor probe system. Thus, the formation of the thiram-Cu2+ complex will speed up the recovery of the red fluorescence and green fluorescence in the trichromatic fluorescence system [6]. However, the decrease in blue fluorescence intensity of the composite system may be due to the large-area overlap between the absorption peak (450 nm) of the thiram-Cu2+ complex and the fluorescence emission peak (429 nm) of B-QDs, it explains the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-QDs may be IFE or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Fig. 5d) [28]. Furthermore, as displayed in Fig. S8(a, g), the fluorescence lifetime of B-QDs and thiram-Cu2+ complex are not significantly lower than that of B-QDs, which further indicates that FRET does not cause fluorescence quenching, so the quenching mechanism of B-QDs is IFE [70]. Therefore, when thiram exists in the reaction system, the color change is that the red and green fluorescence is enhanced, while the blue fluorescence is decreased, to realize the sensitivity and visual detection of thiram.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculation of fluorescence quenching and recovery mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: For further understanding the sensing mechanism of the interaction between tricolor B-CDs/G-QDs/R-QDs fluorescence system and Cu2+ or thiram, as well as the interpret the competitive reaction mechanism of CdTe QDs for thiram and Cu2+, DFT calculation was carried out by Materials Studio. The models of B-QDs, CdTe QDs, and thiram are shown in Fig. S10 (a-c), respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , HOMO and LUMO energies and their spatial distribution are defined. Frontier orbital theory can not only show the active center of material reaction but also show the difficulty of reaction to a certain extent. The lower the LUMO's energy, the better its ability to accept electrons. Conversely, the higher the HOMO's energy, the more capable it is of donating electrons. The difference between the HOMO and the LUMO energy levels can be used to indicate the stability of the compound, the more stable the compound, the lower the energy difference [71]. However, both thiram and CdTe QDs can combine with Cu2+. By comparing the values of △a and △b, it is concluded that Cu2+ is easier to combine with thiram than CdTe QDs. In addition, by calculating the adsorption energy (△Ead) of the reaction between Cu2+ and CdTe QDs, B-QDs, as well as thiram, the △Ead of CdTe QDs-Cu2+ is −3.32392 eV, the △Ead of B-CDs-Cu2+ is −2.51263 eV, and the △Ead of thiram- Cu2+ is −5.91963 eV (Fig. 6f).

The results show that in the absence of thiram, Cu2+ is more likely to bind to CdTe QDs and the electron transfer occurs in the whole detection system, causing the red and green fluorescence to quench, while Cu2+ is hardly to bind to B-QDs, resulting in the blue fluorescence to remain unchanged. When thiram has existed, thiram is easier to complicate with Cu2+ than CdTe QDs, thus blocking the binding of Cu2+ and CdTe QDs, and presenting the recovery of red and green fluorescence. The HOMO-LUMO energy gap reduced from 0.2422 eV to 0.2177 eV before and after the addition of thiram in the mixed group [(B-QDs/CdTe QDs)-Cu2+] system (Fig. 6c).

To further understand the binding ability between B-QDs and CdTe QDs (R-QDs and G-QDs) in the trichromatic fluorescence system, deformation charge density analysis was also performed. The red area represents electron enrichment and the blue area represents electron deficiency. It can be seen from the configuration in Fig. 6d that the positively charged of B-QDs structure is deformed to better combine with the negatively charged CdTe QDs. There is a small overlap in the electron density between them, which proves that there is very weak chemical adsorption, but there is no obvious red region between B-QDs and CdTe QDs, indicating that the main adsorption mode is physical adsorption [72].

It can be seen from Fig. 6e that the S atoms regions of thiram are enriched (red), and the electrons enriched in the Cu2+ region are lost (blue). There is an obvious color difference in the differential charge results, indicating that there is a strong interaction between thiram and Cu2+. The chemical adsorption between the S atom and Cu2+ in the thiram molecule leads to the binding reaction between Cu2+ and thiram, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence of CdTe QDs.

In general, the electrostatic interaction between B-QDs, R-QDs, and G-QDs is mainly due to the adsorption of CdTe QDs around B-QDs, which affects the final occurrence configuration of B-QDs. When there is only Cu2+ in the system, Cu2+ combines with CdTe QDs to produce electron transfer, resulting in the dual-quenching of CdTe QDs (R-QDs, and G-QDs), while the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs isn’t affected. In the presence of thiram, Cu2+ is easier to combine with thiram due to the strong chemical adsorption between Cu2+ and thiram, showing the recovery of CdTe QDs fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based on-site determination of Cu2+ and thiram
ce:section.ce:para: To facilitate the determination of Cu2+ and thiram content, an inexpensive portable device combined with smartphone has been established. Firstly, 20 μL different concentrations of Cu2+ were addition in the B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs (1980 μL). After mixing uniformly, transfer the mixture containing different concentrations of Cu2+ to the glass test tubes, and then put multiple glass test tubes into the test tube groove of the dark box equipment at the same time. Using the smartphone camera to record the fluorescent images and using the self-designed WeChat App to crop and color the images. According to the fluorescent response of different concentrations of Cu2+, a standard curve was constructed. Lastly, the test consequences were shown on the phone. The determination steps of thiram were similar to Cu2+ analysis.

Fluorescent pictures are imported by the built-in camera of the smartphone. As shown in Fig. S13, the optical device is composed of multi-test tube support, a mobile phone card slot, and a detachable upper cover (which can attach an ultraviolet lamp). For the RGB analysis of the images, we have self-designed a WeChat App called “Fluorescence Analysis”, which has the ability of deep learning. As shown in Fig. S14, the App consists of two sections. The first section is using HUAWEI P30 pro and black box to get fluorescent pictures of samples with different concentrations. The image acquisition process adopts the automatic focusing method in the professional model of the smartphone. The shooting parameters for obtaining fluorescence images should be consistent. Firstly, after the program ensures that the input pictures are available, the program uses the YOLO v3 algorithm to realize the intelligent recognition of the fluorescent colorimetric test tube in the image and combines the picture processing algorithm to extract HSV (Hue, Saturation, Value) and RGB (Red, Green, and Blue) signal values. Finally, perform online analysis on the obtained images, calculate signal values, select the best linear relationship and linear range, App will automatically generate the fitting equation and save it. The second section is the detection of Cu2+ or thiram. Collect fluorescent pictures of Cu2+ or thiram of unknown concentration, and use App to obtain signal values. Lastly, the test consequence is acquired according to the linear equation.

In this study, the object detection model of YOLO v3 based on deep learning was used to detect the object of the test tube photos, and the color of each test tube in the photos was automatically extracted (Fig. 7 ). Firstly, manually label the photos, select the position of the test tube. Then, use the YOLO v3 model to learn the labeled photos, the model is based on the structure of convolutional neural networks (CNN) [29]. Its input is the picture of the test tube, and its output is the RGB value of the position and color of the test tube. The model belongs to the end-to-end black-box model, which combines the task of target segmentation and recognition into one task.

A total of 111 fluorescence pictures were collected as data sets, all pictures contain a total of 927 test tubes with fixed positions. it contains a 40% testing set and 60% training set. The pictures contain different fluorescence colors, single cuvette, or multiple cuvettes at different angles, to improve the accuracy of the program reading the target cuvette, as shown in Fig. S15. Expand the sample size of training data utilizing data enhancement, taking photos with different fluorescent colors and different numbers of cuvettes as input, the algorithm marks the useful photos, and the size of the input model image is adjusted to 416*416, and then, the picture uses the label what you see (LWYS) technology to mark all test tube [73]. Annotate the training set as the format VOC2007, and use the YOLO v3 algorithm to predict, and use the Labellmg image annotation tool to mark the essential truth bounding frames, and save the position information of these test tubes in a text file, as shown in Fig. S16. Finally, the position of the test tube in the picture is predicted using the YOLO v3 algorithm. Segment the image according to the boundary position obtained by YOLO v3, and calculate the RGB value of the segmented image. The summary of the model of YOLO v3′s extraction of fluorescence images is shown in Fig. 7. the model of YOLO v3 extracting fluorescence image predicts the global features of the image in the 13 * 13 feature layer and the local features of the image in the 26 * 26 and 52 * 52 feature layers. YOLO v3 also uses the residual network structure, which avoids gradient dispersion when the network is deepened and has an excellent detection effect for target detection [74].

To verify the effectiveness of experiments on the trained YOLO v3 model, the precision-recall (PR) curve and F1-score were applied to appraise the property of the model in the test and recognition of fluorescent test tubes. The definitions of recall, precision, and F1-score are shown in formulas (1)-(3) [75]:
(1)
Pr e c i s i o n =
TP
TP + F P
(2)
Re c a l l =
TP
TP + F N
(3)
F1 =
2 * Precision * Recall
Precision + Recall

Among them, TP denotes the amount of pictures that are the test tube target and the model is correctly detected; FP denotes the amount of pictures that do not belong to the test tube target but are incorrectly detected by the model; FN denotes the amount of pictures that are the test tube but are incorrectly detected by the model quantity.

However, to evaluate the quality of a test tube inspection system, it is important to understand which objects the test tube algorithm misjudged (false positive rate, PNR) [74]:
(4)
FPR = 1 - Percision =
FP
TP + FP

What other test tubes have not been detected (false negative rate, FPR):
(5)
FNR = 1 - Recall =
FN
TP + FN

In summary, the YOLO v3 network model has many parameters. Through calculation, it is found that its precision reaches 92.5%, the recall rate reaches 100.0%, F1 is 96.1%, FPR is 7.5%, and FNR is 0. At the same time, Precision-Recall curves, as shown in Fig. S17, the value of intersection over union (IOU) is 80%, which also shows very excellent results [74]. In this way, the parameter value is acceptable, because the false positive rate of the algorithm and the possibility that no test tube is detected have been proved to be quite small [23].

The picture acquisition methods for establishing a standard linear curve include picture upload and real-time shooting. The picture-upload stage was chosen in this work. As demonstrated in Fig. 8 (direction indicated by red arrow), it gradually changes from orange-red to blue from the test tube. The pictures are automatically recognized after being uploaded to the App, and the fluorescence of the mixture is recorded by the YOLO v3 algorithm because of the addition of Cu, the green and red fluorescence will be reduced, while the blue fluorescence will unchanged the analysis results of App show that the G and R values gradually decrease, and the B value remains unchanged. On this basis, the curves based on Cu2+ concentration and various signal parameters were constructed. As shown in Fig. S18, the good relationships between Cu2+ concentration and H, G, R, B, G/B, R/B, S/V, and (R + G)/B values were constructed. Among them, the linear relationship between R/B and Cu2+ concentration was best with R2 = 0.9940 and has a wide linear range (0.415–10.5 μM), which is similar to that measured by fluorescence instrument. “Fluorescence Analysis” App can quickly analyze Cu2+ concentration by using the R/B value. The test method of thiram is similar to that of Cu2+. Fig. 8 (direction indicated by the blue arrow) shows the change of fluorescence color after adding thiram, the good relationship between thiram concentration and a variety of values was established (Fig. S19). Among them, the linear relationship between thiram concentration and R/B with R2 = 0.9933 and has a wide linear range (1.50–63.00 μM), which is close to the linear range measured by fluorescence instrument.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-based sensor for monitoring of Cu2+ and thiram in real samples
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, selectivity of the tricolor fluorescence sensor for Cu2+and thiram is described in the
supporting information
. Then, this work selected agricultural water, urine, serum, peaches, and cabbage as practical samples to assess the performance of Cu2+ and thiram detection methods by spiking recovery studies. As shown in Table S1 and Table S2, both Cu2+ and thiram can be detected in the selected samples by ICP-MS, HPLC, colorimetric, and the developed tricolor fluorescence sensor, respectively. The recovery rates of sensing of Cu2+ are in the range of 96.05–102.91%, and the relative standard deviation is less than 6.44%. The recovery rates of analysis of thiram are in the range of 94.43–103.65%, and its relative standard deviation is lower than 6.20%. Besides, we also used the smartphone App to test the substance content of the same actual sample. The test flow chart is shown in Fig. S20, the results are shown in Table S3 and Table S4, the recovery rates are 98.45–103.60% and 95.87–102.29% respectively. The consequences obtained confirmed that the proposed method is reliable for the detection of Cu2+ and thiram in practical environments.

In conclusion, we report a deep learning-assisted smartphone-integrated tricolor ratiometric fluorescence optical device for visual monitoring of Cu2+ and thiram. Firstly, the tricolor ratiometric fluorescence probe system is a simple mixture of B-QDs, G-QDs, and R-QDs, which possessed rich color variation. The increase of the content of Cu2+ will lead to the dual-quenching of the fluorescence intensity of R-QDs cand G-QDs based on electron transfer effect, but will not affect the fluorescence intensity of B-QDs, to realize the ratio detection of Cu2+ by the naked eye. Moreover, a dual detection model of colorimetric and ratiometric for monitoring the concentration of thiram based on the complexing effect and inner filter effect. In brief, the B-QDs/G-QDs/R-QDs tricolor fluorescence system based on artificial intelligence equipment exhibits excellent stability, specificity, and sensitivity, which provides a good potential for the convenient evaluation of Cu2+ concentration and thiram residue. In addition, experimental results from real-time fluorescent probe readings and other remotely collected data can be combined with literature data as input to complex predictive models. Deep learning can also help solve pollution problems in agricultural environments by making estimates based on predicted species, or concentration of taste and odor compounds. However, this basic research also faces the difficulties of entering the market, such as the promotion of devices and smartphone applets. The practical application of basic research is still a huge gap we need to cross.
